title: Building Autonomous AI Assistants
subtitle: Personal AI that gets actual work done — for solo founders, knowledge workers, and small teams
description: A practical course on building AI assistants that operate autonomously on your behalf — reading email, scheduling, drafting docs, taking actions across your tools. Not a chatbot. A coworker. Built from the patterns Anthropic, OpenAI, and the early autonomous-agent products are converging on: connectors, MCP, scheduled tasks, memory, approval flows, and the safety patterns that let an AI act in the real world.
audience: Builders shipping AI assistants — solo founders extending themselves, teams automating internal workflows, product teams making AI a teammate rather than a tool. Comfortable with code; have shipped at least one LLM feature; understand agent loops.
prerequisites: Have shipped at least one AI feature (chat, RAG, or agent)
Familiarity with the agent loop (recommend the AI Agent Engineering course in this catalog first)
API keys for at least one major LLM provider (Anthropic or OpenAI)
outcomes: Define what 'autonomous' means in practical product terms and pick the right autonomy level
Connect an assistant to real tools (calendar, email, docs, code) safely
Implement scheduled and background tasks that run on the user's behalf
Build durable memory and context that survives sessions
Design approval and notification flows that keep users in control
Apply safety, privacy, and audit patterns specific to assistants that act on user accounts
Ship an autonomous assistant to real users without losing sleep

modules.title: What an Autonomous Assistant Actually Is
modules.summary: Define autonomy along a spectrum, distinguish assistants from chatbots and agents, and pick the right level for your product.

lessons.title: The autonomy spectrum — from chatbot to coworker
lessons.objective: Place your product on the autonomy spectrum and pick the right level.

lessons.title: Assistant vs. agent vs. chatbot — naming things clearly
lessons.objective: Distinguish the three patterns and what users expect from each.

lessons.title: The trust budget — what users will let an assistant do
lessons.objective: Map the actions you propose against user trust levels.

lessons.title: Scoping the first version
lessons.objective: Cut the assistant's scope to something users will actually adopt.

modules.title: Connecting the Assistant to the Real World
modules.summary: Connectors, MCP, OAuth, and the integration patterns that make an assistant useful beyond chat.

lessons.title: Connectors — how assistants plug into your tools
lessons.objective: Set up safe connections to calendar, email, docs, code, and chat.

lessons.title: MCP for assistants — your tool ecosystem
lessons.objective: Use Model Context Protocol to standardize tool access.

lessons.title: OAuth and permission scoping in practice
lessons.objective: Get the right permissions; ask for nothing more.

lessons.title: Tool inventory and surface area
lessons.objective: Decide what the assistant can and can't touch — and why.

modules.title: Scheduled and Background Tasks
modules.summary: Assistants that work while you sleep — daily briefings, scheduled actions, background research.

lessons.title: Why scheduled tasks change the product
lessons.objective: Recognize how time-shifted execution reshapes the assistant relationship.

lessons.title: Recurring tasks — patterns for daily briefings, weekly digests
lessons.objective: Build cron-like scheduling with sane defaults and user controls.

lessons.title: Event-triggered tasks — react to inbox, calendar, signals
lessons.objective: Wire assistants to real-world events without spamming users.

lessons.title: Notifications and digests — surfacing background work
lessons.objective: Tell users what happened without overwhelming them.

modules.title: Memory, Context, and Continuity
modules.summary: Make the assistant feel like it knows you — and avoid the failure modes of memory done badly.

lessons.title: Working memory across sessions
lessons.objective: Carry context from one interaction to the next without re-asking.

lessons.title: Long-term memory and personalization
lessons.objective: Build a user profile + episodic memory that gets richer over time.

lessons.title: Context retrieval for assistants
lessons.objective: Pull the right context at the right moment without flooding the prompt.

lessons.title: When the assistant should forget
lessons.objective: Decide what to expire, what to confirm, what to delete.

modules.title: Safety, Approval, and Shipping
modules.summary: The safety patterns that let an assistant act on real user accounts, plus the launch checklist for a first autonomous product.

lessons.title: Approval flows — how to ask, when to act unilaterally
lessons.objective: Design approval UX that respects user agency without becoming friction.

lessons.title: Transparency — making the assistant's work visible
lessons.objective: Surface what the assistant is doing so users keep trust.

lessons.title: Privacy, audit, and the user's right to know
lessons.objective: Apply the privacy and audit controls specific to assistant products.

lessons.title: Launching an autonomous assistant — the early-adopter playbook
lessons.objective: Roll out an autonomous product safely with the right user cohort.
